name: Whey (food medium)
basis: per 100 g fluid sweet whey (cheese/rennet whey)

components.name: lactose
components.source: USDA FDC 171282 (Whey, sweet, fluid, SR Legacy)
components.evidence: Total carbohydrate = total sugars = 5.14 g/100g; lactose is essentially the sole sugar of whey and the dominant fermentable carbon/energy source. Literature sweet-whey lactose 46-52 g/L is consistent (21 CFR 184.1979).

components.name: D-glucose
components.evidence: Only trace free glucose in fresh sweet whey (from residual lactose hydrolysis); not separately quantified by USDA. Included as null-present.

components.name: D-galactose
components.evidence: Trace free galactose in sweet whey; negligible before microbial lactose fermentation. Null-present.

components.name: citric acid
components.source: Milk citrate ~1.75-2.0 g/L, ~90% soluble and partitions to whey during cheesemaking (cheesescientist.com; PMC9457946)
components.evidence: Milk citrate (1.75-2.0 g/L, mostly soluble) passes almost entirely into the aqueous whey; sweet whey citric acid ~1.6 g/L = 0.16 g/100g. A dominant native organic acid of sweet whey, absent from the FooDB profile.

components.name: lactic acid
components.source: 21 CFR 184.1979 (sweet whey titratable acidity <=0.16% as lactic); J Dairy Sci review S0022-0302(20)31055-9
components.evidence: Sweet whey lactic acid is low (~0.5 g/L, range up to 1.6 g/L at the 0.16% acidity limit) versus acid whey which has up to ~16x more (up to ~8 g/L). Key omission from FooDB; rises rapidly if whey ferments.

components.name: orotic acid
components.source: J Dairy Sci permeate organic-acid HPLC study S0022-0302(14)00494-9
components.evidence: Orotic acid is a characteristic minor dairy organic acid (~0.06-0.10 g/L in milk/whey); partitions into whey. Medium confidence on the exact figure.

components.name: acetic acid
components.source: J Dairy Sci permeate organic-acid HPLC study S0022-0302(14)00494-9
components.evidence: Trace acetic acid reported among whey/permeate organic acids; low concentration in fresh sweet whey. Low confidence, trace.

components.name: total protein (whey proteins)
components.evidence: Predominantly beta-lactoglobulin and alpha-lactalbumin plus proteose-peptones; 6-9 g/L in sweet whey (21 CFR 184.1979). Amino-acid rows below are the protein-derived profile.

components.name: L-tryptophan
components.evidence: Protein-derived amino-acid profile of sweet whey.

components.name: L-cystine
components.evidence: Protein-derived profile; whey proteins are cysteine-rich.

components.name: L-glutamate
components.evidence: Protein-derived profile (glutamic acid); most abundant amino acid.

components.name: chloride
components.source: dairy composition literature (sweet whey Cl ~1.1 g/L)
components.evidence: Not in USDA panel; whey chloride ~1.0-1.4 g/L. Medium confidence.

components.name: iodine
components.evidence: Milk/whey contain iodine (highly variable, ~50-250 ug/L in milk; partitions to whey). Present, not quantified by USDA for whey. Null-present.

components.name: riboflavin (vitamin B2)
components.evidence: Whey is riboflavin-rich (gives its greenish-yellow color).

components.name: pantothenic acid (vitamin B5)
components.evidence: Water-soluble B vitamin retained in whey.

components.name: vitamin B12 (cobalamin)
components.evidence: Dairy-derived cobalamin; supplies the food's cobalt.

components.name: total fat
components.evidence: Whey is nearly fat-free; residual milk fat only.

components.name: saturated fatty acids (total)
components.evidence: Dominant lipid fraction; incl. myristic (0.035), palmitic (0.110), stearic (0.033) g/100g.

components.name: cholesterol
components.evidence: Low but genuine; whey is an animal (dairy) product, so cholesterol is a real trace component (kept, unlike the plant-food case).

components.name: water
components.evidence: Whey is ~93% water; the bulk medium solvent.

remove.name: Sucrose C12H22O11
remove.reason: Whey contains no sucrose; the sole sugar is lactose. FooDB mis-assignment.

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: Non-nutritive trace/contaminant metal, not a defining whey nutrient.

remove.name: Co2+
remove.reason: Elemental cobalt is present only within vitamin B12; standalone Co2+ is non-nutritive FooDB noise (B12 retained).

remove.name: Molybdate
remove.reason: Ultratrace, unquantified; not a growth-relevant whey nutrient.

remove.name: (R)-Oxypeucedanin
remove.reason: Plant furanocoumarin (parsnip/citrus); biologically implausible in dairy whey. FooDB predicted noise.

remove.name: epsilon-Polylysine
remove.reason: Microbial-fermentation preservative, not a native whey constituent. FooDB noise.

remove.name: (E)-2-Phenyl-2-butenal
remove.reason: Trace flavor aldehyde/volatile, not a growth nutrient.

remove.name: (±)-erythro-Isoleucine
remove.reason: Non-physiological isoleucine stereoisomer artifact; real isoleucine already listed as L-isoleucine.

remove.name: 24,25-Dihydroxyvitamin D3
remove.reason: Predicted trace vitamin-D metabolite, not a measured growth nutrient of whey.

missing_added: citric acid (~0.16 g/100g) — dominant native whey organic acid, absent from FooDB
lactic acid (~0.05 g/100g sweet whey; up to ~1.6 g/L, far higher in acid whey)
orotic acid and acetic acid (characteristic dairy organic acids)
lactose given an explicit quantitative amount (5.14 g/100g) as the dominant fermentable sugar
full protein-derived amino-acid profile with amounts
keep_uncovered: beta-Lactose (anomer of lactose; chemically the same sugar already covered)
Myristoleic acid (C14:1) — genuine trace milk fatty acid, no dedicated BiGG met
beta-Carotene / retinol precursors from residual milk fat (genuine trace)
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 171282 (Whey, sweet, fluid; SR Legacy) — macronutrients, sugars, amino acids, fatty acids, minerals, vitamins, cholesterol
21 CFR 184.1979 (Whey standard) — sweet-whey lactose 46-52 g/L, protein 6-9 g/L, titratable acidity <=0.16% as lactic
J Dairy Sci invited review, Acid whey trends and health benefits, S0022-0302(20)31055-9 — sweet vs acid whey lactic acid
J Dairy Sci, role of sodium in salty taste of permeate, S0022-0302(14)00494-9 — whey/permeate organic acids (lactic, citric, orotic)
Milk citrate partition (~1.75-2.0 g/L, ~90% soluble) into whey — cheesescientist.com; PMC9457946
notes: Sweet (rennet/cheese) fluid whey. Dominant carbon/energy source is lactose (~5.1 g/100g, ~150 mM); the growth-critical additions over the FooDB profile are the organic acids — citric acid (~0.16 g/100g, ~8 mM, inherited from milk citrate) and lactic acid (low in sweet whey but the defining acid of acid/yogurt whey, up to ~16x higher). Secondary N sources are soluble whey proteins (beta-lactoglobulin, alpha-lactalbumin) and their amino acids; minerals dominated by K > Na > Ca > P; riboflavin-rich. Nearly fat-free.
